title: Out of the Dark Ages
bullets: After the Mycenaean collapse around 1200 BCE, Greece lost writing, monumental architecture, and international trade for three centuries — population in some regions dropped by 90 percent.
Around 800 BCE the Greeks adapted the Phoenician consonant-only script by adding vowels, creating the first true alphabet and the ancestor of every Latin and Cyrillic writing system in use today.

Homer composed the Iliad and Odyssey around 750 BCE, providing the fractious Greek city-states with a shared cultural curriculum, common mythology, and a heroic vocabulary that defined Greek identity.
The polis, a self-governing political community defined by citizenship rather than walls, emerged as a new form of political organization unlike anything else in the ancient world.
Starting around 750 BCE, Greek colonists founded independent city-states from Massalia to Byzantion to Syracuse, scattering Greek culture across the Mediterranean and Black Sea coasts.

The first recorded Olympic Games at Olympia in 776 BCE established a four-year cycle of pan-Hellenic festivals that united fiercely independent city-states through sacred truces and shared competition.
The Oracle at Delphi, where the Pythia delivered ambiguous prophecies while seated over ethylene-emitting rock fissures, became the most influential religious institution in the Greek world.
The Eleusinian Mysteries, secret rites honoring Demeter and Persephone that promised initiates a blessed afterlife, were practiced for over a thousand years and their rituals remain unknown.

title: Tyrants and Lawmakers
bullets: Most early Greek poleis were governed by aristocratic landowners who kept laws unwritten, monopolized courts, and could legally sell debt-ridden citizens into slavery.
The hoplite phalanx, a dense formation of heavily armed citizen-soldiers fighting shoulder-to-shoulder, created a new military class that demanded political voice to match their battlefield role.
Draco produced Athens' first written law code in 621 BCE, so harsh that later Athenians said it was written in blood — the word draconian still means excessively severe.
Solon cancelled all debts in 594 BCE, freed enslaved Athenians, banned using personal freedom as loan collateral, and created four wealth-based political classes with defined rights.
Peisistratos seized power as tyrant in 561 BCE, commissioned the first official written text of Homer, and established the Great Dionysia, the dramatic festival that birthed Greek theater.
Cleisthenes in 508 BCE invented demokratia by breaking aristocratic power, creating ten cross-regional tribes, establishing the Council of 500 chosen by lot, and introducing ostracism.
In 508 BCE, every other major civilization on earth was governed by kings or emperors — Athens' idea that every citizen had an equal vote in the sovereign assembly was genuinely unprecedented.
Sparta enslaved the entire population of neighboring Messenia as helots, then built a society organized entirely around military readiness, with boys entering state training at age seven.
Thales of Miletus around 585 BCE became the first person in the surviving record to propose natural explanations for natural phenomena, launching the tradition of Greek philosophy.

Pythagoras founded a philosophical commune of mathematicians at Croton around 530 BCE who believed the universe was structured by number and proved the theorem that bears his name from first principles.

title: The Persian Wars
bullets: The Ionian Revolt of 499 BCE began when Greek cities on the Anatolian coast rebelled against Persia; Athens sent ships that helped burn the Persian provincial capital of Sardis.
At Marathon in 490 BCE roughly 10,000 Athenian hoplites defeated a Persian punitive force twice their size using a double envelopment, proving that Greek heavy infantry could beat the Persian Empire.
Themistocles convinced the Athenian assembly to spend a silver windfall from the Laurium mines on 200 triremes instead of a cash dividend — the fleet that would save Greece at Salamis.
King Leonidas and 300 Spartans held the pass at Thermopylae for two days against Xerxes' entire army until a local traitor revealed a mountain path; all 300 Spartans died.

Themistocles lured the Persian fleet into the narrow strait of Salamis, where their superior numbers became a liability, and Greek triremes rammed them at close quarters in the decisive naval battle of the war.
At Plataea in 479 BCE a combined Greek army of 40,000 hoplites under the Spartan general Pausanias destroyed the remaining Persian army and killed its commander Mardonius.
Herodotus composed his Histories around 440 BCE to explain the Greek victory over Persia, producing the first major work of narrative history and earning the title Father of History.
Athens emerged from the Persian Wars as a naval superpower that had provided the fleet at Salamis, giving it a sense of destiny and imperial ambition that would define the next half-century.

title: The Golden Age of Athens
bullets: The Delian League, formed in 478 BCE as a voluntary anti-Persian alliance, was transformed within twenty years into an Athenian protection racket that crushed revolts and confiscated allied treasuries.
Pericles dominated Athenian politics for over thirty years, using allied tribute to fund the building projects that made Athens the most architecturally spectacular city in Greece.

The Parthenon, completed in 432 BCE, contained deliberate optical refinements so precise that its columns lean inward, its floor curves upward, and its horizontal lines are subtly curved to appear straight.
Phidias created two of antiquity's most celebrated artworks: the chryselephantine Athena in the Parthenon (38 feet tall, consuming 2,500 pounds of gold) and the Statue of Zeus at Olympia.

Aeschylus, Sophocles, and Euripides produced Greek tragedy at the Great Dionysia, where theater functioned as a civic institution that engaged 17,000 spectators with the political and moral questions of the day.
Aristophanes wrote satirical comedies that lampooned Athenian politicians and philosophers — his Lysistrata imagined women withholding sex until their husbands stopped fighting the Peloponnesian War.
Socrates revolutionized philosophy by teaching through relentless questioning, probing assumptions about justice, courage, and virtue in the Athenian Agora without ever writing a single word.
Democritus proposed that all matter is composed of tiny indivisible atomoi moving through void, a theory confirmed over two thousand years later by modern physics.

Hippocrates of Kos founded rational medicine, insisting that diseases have natural causes and should be treated through observation rather than prayer — the Hippocratic Oath is still taken by doctors today.

title: The Peloponnesian War
bullets: Thucydides identified the fundamental cause of the Peloponnesian War with brutal clarity: the growth of Athenian power and the alarm it inspired in Sparta made war inevitable.
Pericles' strategy was to abandon the countryside, pack the population behind the Long Walls, and use the fleet and imperial tribute to outlast Sparta — the overcrowding proved catastrophic.
A devastating plague struck overcrowded Athens in 430 BCE, killing perhaps a quarter of the population including Pericles himself, undoing the very strategy that was supposed to protect the city.
Alcibiades persuaded Athens to launch the Sicilian Expedition in 415 BCE — roughly 40,000 Athenians and allies were killed or enslaved when the operation ended in catastrophe at Syracuse.
Sparta won the war at sea using Persian gold to build a fleet, while Athens briefly overthrew its own democracy in 411 BCE under the stress of defeat.
Lysander destroyed the Athenian fleet at Aegospotami in 405 BCE, capturing almost all 180 triremes; without its fleet Athens could not protect its Black Sea grain supply and starved.
Athens surrendered in 404 BCE: the Long Walls were torn down to flute music, the fleet was reduced to twelve ships, and the oligarchic Thirty Tyrants were installed under Spartan supervision.
The Peloponnesian War killed roughly half of Athens' male citizen population and demonstrated that the Greek city-state system contained a fatal flaw: the poleis could not stop fighting each other.

title: Crisis and Philip
bullets: Sparta won the Peloponnesian War and proved spectacularly incapable of running an empire — within a decade it was more hated than Athens had ever been.
Epaminondas reinvented infantry tactics at Leuctra in 371 BCE with the oblique phalanx, concentrating fifty shields deep on one wing to crush the Spartan elite for the first time in a pitched battle.
Epaminondas liberated the helots of Messenia after three centuries of enslavement, permanently crippling the Spartan military economy that depended on their labor.

Athens executed Socrates in 399 BCE on charges of impiety and corrupting the youth; he chose death over exile, arguing that a philosopher who fled to save his skin would have nothing left worth saving.
Plato founded the Academy in 387 BCE, the Western world's first institution of higher learning, which operated continuously for nearly 900 years until Justinian closed it in 529 CE.
Plato's Republic explored justice and the ideal state through the Allegory of the Cave — prisoners mistaking shadows for reality, the philosopher as the one who escapes into sunlight.

Philip II of Macedon created the sarissa-armed phalanx combined with heavy cavalry, then spent twenty years dismantling the Greek city-state system through military pressure and diplomatic manipulation.
At Chaeronea in 338 BCE Philip's army destroyed the combined Athenian-Theban force and annihilated the Sacred Band of Thebes, ending Greek city-state independence forever.

Aristotle, Plato's student and Alexander's tutor, founded the Lyceum in 335 BCE and wrote systematically on logic, ethics, biology, politics, and poetics — his work shaped Western thought for two millennia.

title: Alexander
bullets: Alexander crossed into Asia in 334 BCE with 37,000 soldiers, a copy of Homer's Iliad annotated by Aristotle, and the stated intention of conquering the Persian Empire — he was twenty-two.
At the Battle of Issus in 333 BCE Alexander drove straight at Darius III with the Companion cavalry, sending the Great King fleeing and capturing his family, war chest, and royal tent.
The seven-month siege of Tyre in 332 BCE required Alexander to build a half-mile causeway to the island fortress, permanently turning Tyre from an island into a peninsula.
At Gaugamela in 331 BCE Alexander used combined-arms tactics to destroy the Persian army on ground Darius had chosen, leading a wedge cavalry charge through a gap in the Persian line.
Alexander burned Persepolis, the ceremonial heart of the Achaemenid Empire, in an act debated as revenge for Xerxes' burning of Athens, a drunken accident, or a calculated political statement.

Alexander pushed east for seven years through Afghanistan, Central Asia, and into the Indus Valley before his army mutinied at the Hydaspes and refused to march further — they were 3,000 miles from home.
The return march through the Gedrosian Desert in 325 BCE killed an estimated quarter to third of the army from thirst, heat, and starvation.
Alexander died at Babylon in June 323 BCE at age thirty-two after a prolonged fever, leaving no succession plan and reportedly saying his empire should go to the strongest.

title: The Hellenistic World and Rome
bullets: Alexander's generals fought forty years of brutal wars for his empire, murdering both his heirs before stabilizing into three successor kingdoms: Ptolemaic Egypt, the Seleucid Empire, and Antigonid Macedon.
The Library of Alexandria, founded under Ptolemy I around 295 BCE, may have held up to 400,000 scrolls and functioned as the world's first state-funded research university.
Euclid compiled the Elements around 300 BCE, systematizing geometry into axioms and proofs — the textbook remained the standard for over two thousand years.
Archimedes of Syracuse calculated pi, discovered the principle of buoyancy, invented siege engines, and was killed by a Roman soldier while drawing a mathematical diagram in the sand.
Eratosthenes calculated Earth's circumference within 2 percent of the modern value using nothing more than the angle of shadows in two cities at noon on the summer solstice.

The Antikythera Mechanism, built around 200 BCE and recovered from a shipwreck in 1901, is a bronze analog computer that tracked astronomical positions — technology not matched for over a thousand years.
Stoicism, founded by Zeno of Citium around 300 BCE, taught that virtue is the only true good and external circumstances are indifferent — it became the dominant philosophy of the Roman world.
Rome destroyed Corinth in 146 BCE, ending Greek political independence, but Greek culture conquered Rome: Roman aristocrats spoke Greek, studied Greek philosophy, and copied Greek art.
Cleopatra VII, the last Ptolemaic ruler, died in 30 BCE after the Battle of Actium, ending the Hellenistic age that Alexander had opened three centuries earlier.
